slides.headline: It's not your sound.
slides.eyebrow: Education · 5 reasons African music gets rejected in sync
fit_warnings: [01 hook.eyebrow] copy (57 chars) exceeds slot budget (48 at 44px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body: Here are the actual reasons African composers get passed over in sync licensing — none of them have anything to do with talent. ↓

REASON 1: UNCLEARED SAMPLES
Even a small sample that isn't cleared makes the track legally untouchable.
Productions carry full liability for anything they broadcast.
They won't risk it, no matter how good the track is.
fit_warnings: [03 context.body] copy (215 chars) exceeds slot budget (206 at 48px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body:
REASON 2: SPLIT RIGHTS
Label owns your master. You own publishing.
Two separate negotiations for one placement.
Most supervisors have 20 other options. They move on.

REASON 3: NO METADATA
No BPM tag. No ISRC. No mood categorisation.
Your track doesn't show up in a search.
You're invisible even when you're technically "in the library."

REASON 4: WRONG FILE FORMAT
MP3 files. SoundCloud links. Google Drive folders.
Supervisors need lossless files, properly named, ready immediately. The work, in numbers. → Why none of this is about your sound
→ The sample clearance issue and what to do about it
→ Rights splits — how to protect yourself before signing anything
→ The metadata checklist for every file in your catalogue
→ File format requirements for professional submissions
→ Building a 24-hour response system that actually works Save this. Fix what you can. Then apply. #SyncReady #AfricanComposers #SyncMaster #SyncLicensing #MusicBusiness
